why: delete_api_key is called with caller-supplied api_key_id without verifying that the key belongs to the authenticated user, allowing any authenticated user to delete any other user's API key.

build_flow (UNIT 18) is referenced internally by build_flow_and_stream without an auth dependency on the route decorator, and UNIT 18's decorator has no dependencies=[Depends(get_current_active_user)], relying solely on the CurrentActiveUser parameter which may not enforce auth on all paths.

The router.post('/build/{flow_id}/flow') decorator has no dependencies=[Depends(get_current_active_user)]; authentication relies only on the CurrentActiveUser parameter injection which may be bypassable if the dependency is not enforced at the router level.
get_build_events fetches events for a caller-supplied job_id with no check that the job belongs to the authenticated user, allowing any authenticated user to poll another user's build events.
cancel_flow_build is called with a caller-supplied job_id with no ownership check, allowing any authenticated user to cancel another user's build job.

The deprecated build_vertex route decorator has no dependencies=[Depends(get_current_active_user)], relying only on CurrentActiveUser parameter; if the dependency resolution is misconfigured this endpoint would be unauthenticated.
traceback.format_exc() is captured and included in the 'stackTrace' field of the response message returned to the client, exposing internal stack traces from build failures.

webhook_run_flow has no authentication dependency (no Depends(get_current_user) or Depends(api_key_security)); get_webhook_user may permit unauthenticated execution when auth is disabled, allowing anyone to trigger arbitrary flow execution.

create_upload_file accepts a file upload scoped to a caller-supplied flow_id but has no authentication dependency (no Depends(get_current_active_user) or ownership check), so any unauthenticated caller can upload files into any flow's folder.

The Content-Disposition header value is built by interpolating the caller-supplied file_name directly into an f-string without stripping CR/LF or quoting the filename token, enabling header injection if ValidatedFileName does not strip newline characters.

Component(_code=raw_code.code) accepts raw Python source code from the authenticated request body and passes it to a component builder that compiles and executes it, constituting server-side code injection from external input.

Component(_code=code_request.code) accepts raw Python source code from the request body and passes it to build_custom_component_template which compiles/executes it, constituting server-side code injection from external input.
The SQLAlchemy StatementError exception (which can embed SQL query text or connection details) is converted with str(exc) and returned directly as the HTTPException detail to the client.

The download_image endpoint has no authentication dependency (no Depends(get_current_user) or equivalent), allowing any unauthenticated caller to retrieve arbitrary stored images by supplying a flow_id and file_name.
The download_profile_picture endpoint has no authentication dependency, permitting unauthenticated access to profile pictures on the filesystem.
The list_profile_pictures endpoint has no authentication dependency, allowing any unauthenticated caller to enumerate profile picture filenames from the server.

The read_public_flow endpoint has no authentication dependency and the access_type check uses 'is not' comparison against an enum value (AccessTypeEnum.PUBLIC) rather than '!=' which could silently fail to restrict non-public flows, potentially exposing any flow's data without authentication.

The Content-Disposition header value is built using an f-string interpolating 'filename' derived from a server-generated timestamp string, but the flow name (from user-controlled data) is used as the zip entry name without being sanitized of CR/LF or header-breaking characters; however the filename itself here is time-based so this is low risk — the more relevant concern is the flow name used in writestr at line 637 could contain special characters, but the actual header at line 649 uses only the timestamp so this is borderline.
The read_basic_examples endpoint has no authentication dependency, allowing any unauthenticated caller to retrieve all starter folder flows and their full data.
The error detail at line 96 includes the full resolved base_dir_resolved path (an absolute filesystem path) in the HTTP 400 response, leaking internal directory structure to the caller.

The error detail at lines 103-105 interpolates both the raw OSError message (which may embed absolute paths or OS-level details) and the full base_dir_resolved filesystem path into the HTTP response body.

The 'search' query parameter is interpolated directly into the redirect_url without encoding or stripping of CR/LF or URL-breaking characters, and this URL is then used as the Location header value in a RedirectResponse, enabling header injection if search contains newline characters.
The delete_folder_redirect handler has no auth dependency (no Depends(get_current_active_user) or equivalent), allowing any unauthenticated caller to trigger a DELETE redirect for any folder_id.
The download_file_redirect handler has no auth dependency, allowing unauthenticated callers to trigger download redirects for arbitrary folder IDs.
kb_name is taken directly from the URL path and joined onto kb_root_path / kb_user without resolve() + containment check, so a value like '../../etc' can escape the user's directory.
kb_name from the URL path is joined onto kb_root_path / kb_user and then passed to shutil.rmtree without a realpath/resolve containment check, enabling directory traversal deletion.
Each kb_name in request.kb_names is joined onto kb_user_path without resolve() + containment check before being passed to shutil.rmtree, enabling traversal-based arbitrary directory deletion.
kb_user is derived from current_user.username and joined onto kb_root_path without path containment validation; a username containing path separators could escape the intended root directory.
user.store_api_key is set as a cookie value in plaintext via set_cookie, exposing the raw API key to the client.
user.store_api_key is set as a plaintext cookie value, exposing the raw API key to the client without encryption or redaction.
The auto_login endpoint has no authentication dependency and issues long-term tokens when AUTO_LOGIN is enabled, allowing any unauthenticated client to obtain valid credentials.
The BrokenResourceError exception message is interpolated directly into the HTTPException detail returned to the client, potentially leaking internal connection or transport details.
The broad except block converts the raw exception to str(e) and passes it as the HTTPException detail, which may expose internal ORM, filesystem, or connection-string details to the caller.

If an exception is raised after auth_settings are decrypted (line 277) but before masking is complete, str(e) surfaced in the 500 response could contain decrypted OAuth client secrets or API keys from the exception context.
The BrokenResourceError exception message is interpolated into the HTTPException detail returned to the client, potentially leaking internal transport or connection details.

On an unexpected Exception during MCP Composer startup, `str(e)` is passed directly as the HTTPException detail, which may embed internal paths, connection strings, or other sensitive context from lower-level libraries.

The client IP is taken verbatim from the attacker-controlled X-Forwarded-For header without validating that the request actually arrived through a trusted proxy, allowing a remote attacker to spoof a loopback address and bypass the local-only check in install_mcp_config.

The local-IP guard in install_mcp_config relies on get_client_ip which trusts X-Forwarded-For, so a remote attacker can set that header to '127.0.0.1' and invoke the install endpoint from any network.
The outer except block passes `str(e)` as the HTTPException detail, potentially leaking filesystem paths or internal error messages from file-IO operations on the config file.
The outer except block passes `str(e)` as the HTTPException detail, potentially surfacing internal DB or filesystem error messages to the caller.

The Windows username obtained from cmd.exe stdout is decoded and interpolated directly into a filesystem path without sanitization; a username containing '../' or an absolute path segment could escape the intended /mnt/c/Users/ directory.

The filename is extracted from a caller-supplied URI via unquote() and passed directly to storage_service.get_file() without any containment check against a fixed base path, allowing '../' sequences to escape the intended directory.

The raw exception message from a flow execution (which may include SQL, file paths, or internal service details) is appended directly into TextContent returned to the MCP caller, leaking internal error details to external clients.
The exception string from an ORM/execution layer is interpolated into a TextContent result sent back to the MCP client, potentially exposing SQL queries, file paths, or connection strings.

The caller-supplied `order_by` string is passed directly to `getattr(MessageTable, order_by)` without any allowlist validation, allowing an attacker to supply arbitrary attribute names that could cause unexpected query behavior or errors leaking schema information; while not classic string concatenation, dynamic attribute resolution from untrusted input is semantically equivalent in risk.

The `delete_messages` handler deletes MessageTable rows by caller-supplied `message_ids` with no check that the authenticated user owns those messages, allowing any authenticated user to delete any other user's messages.
The `update_message` handler fetches and mutates a message solely by the caller-supplied `message_id` path parameter without verifying the message belongs to the authenticated user.
The `update_session_id` handler fetches and updates all messages matching the caller-supplied `old_session_id` without checking that those messages belong to the authenticated user.

The `delete_messages_session` handler deletes all messages matching the caller-supplied `session_id` without verifying ownership, allowing any authenticated user to delete another user's session messages.
The `get_transactions` handler queries TransactionTable rows solely by the caller-supplied `flow_id` without confirming the flow belongs to the authenticated user, enabling cross-user data access.

In the streaming error handler, `str(e)` from an internal exception (potentially from DB, filesystem, or flow execution) is placed directly in the SSE stream sent to the client via `create_openai_error(message=str(e), ...)`, potentially leaking internal paths, SQL, or connection details.

The top-level exception handler passes `str(exc)` from an arbitrary internal exception directly into the OpenAI error response returned to the client, potentially leaking internal implementation details from DB/ORM, network, or deserialization errors.
On decryption failure the fallback returns the raw (undecrypted) store_api_key value to the caller instead of None or a safe sentinel, exposing the stored secret.

The flows query on line 604 filters only by folder_id without also filtering by user_id, so an authenticated user who owns the project folder can retrieve flows belonging to other users if the folder_id is known.

The Content-Disposition header is built from project.name (a user-controlled database value); although URL-encoded via quote(), the RFC 5987 encoded filename* value still embeds the raw project name in the unencoded filename= fallback position if one were added, and more critically the project name is not stripped of CR/LF before being incorporated into filename on line 622, so a project name containing newline characters could inject additional headers.

The exception from validate_code (which may process user-supplied code and invoke compilation/import machinery) is stringified and returned verbatim as the HTTP 500 detail, potentially exposing internal paths, module names, or syntax error details to the caller.

The exception from process_prompt_template on user-supplied template/name fields is stringified and returned verbatim as the HTTP 500 detail, potentially leaking internal implementation details to the caller.

update_variable_fields is called with the caller-supplied variable_id and the current user's id, but it is not verified that the variable identified by variable_id belongs to current_user before the update is performed; the ownership check is delegated to the service layer which may not enforce it.

delete_variable_by_id is called with the caller-supplied variable_id and current_user.id but there is no explicit ownership verification in the handler confirming the variable belongs to the authenticated user before deletion.

The JSON decode exception message (e!s) is forwarded verbatim to the OpenAI/client channel as function_call_output, potentially exposing internal parsing details or data snippets to the downstream consumer.

The ConnectionError/WebSocketException string (e!s) is forwarded verbatim to the client as function_call_output, potentially exposing internal host/port or connection-string details from the exception message.

The KeyError/AttributeError/TypeError exception string is forwarded verbatim to the client as function_call_output, potentially exposing internal attribute names, key names, or data-structure details.

exec() is called on func_body (line 298-323) built by interpolating input_.display_name and input_.display_name-derived strings (lines 280, 293-295) from flow vertex objects that a user can name arbitrarily, allowing an attacker who controls a flow input's display_name to inject arbitrary Python into the compiled function body and execute it with globals() scope.

flow_id is taken directly from the WebSocket path parameter (line 718) and passed to get_flow_desc_from_db without any check that the authenticated current_user owns the flow, allowing any authenticated user to probe or execute arbitrary flows by supplying a foreign flow_id.

flow_id from the path parameter (line 1146) is passed directly to build_flow_and_stream (line 1255) with no prior check that the authenticated current_user owns that flow, enabling any authenticated user to trigger execution of another user's flow.

The requests.RequestException (a specific, non-broad exception type) caught at line 1368 is surfaced verbatim to the caller via return {"error": str(e)} at line 1370; RequestException messages from the requests library routinely embed full URLs, host names, and port numbers, leaking internal infrastructure details to the client.

SYSTEMIC NOTE (one instance reported): the exception from the database call get_flow_desc_from_db is forwarded to the WebSocket client as {"error": f"Failed to load flow: {e!s}"} at line 748; database-layer exceptions frequently embed SQL fragments, table names, or connection strings, and this pattern recurs across multiple except-Exception blocks that send_json to the client throughout this file.
